% Auto-generated. Do not edit by hand.
\section*{Quantum Volume}

We benchmark Quantum Volume following \href{https://journals.aps.org/pra/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevA.100.032328}{Cross et al., PRA 100, 032328 (2019)}. For each width \(n\), we generate square model circuits of width \(n\) and depth \(n\), and measure the heavy-output probability \(h\), namely the total probability assigned to outcomes whose ideal probabilities are above the median of the ideal output distribution.

A width is considered to pass if the heavy-output probability exceeds the threshold \(2/3\) according to a statistical lower bound. For runs with at least \(100\) completed trials, we use the pooled confidence criterion of Cross et al. If \(n_c\) is the number of completed circuits, \(n_s\) the number of shots per circuit, \(n_h\) the total number of heavy outputs observed across all trials, and \(\sigma\) the chosen confidence parameter, we use
\[
h_{\mathrm{LB}}
=
\frac{n_h - \sigma \sqrt{\,n_h\!\left(n_c n_s - n_h/n_c\right)}}{n_c n_s},
\]
and declare the width successful when \(h_{\mathrm{LB}} > 2/3\). For smaller runs, we use the simpler normal approximation
\[
h_{\mathrm{LB}} = \bar{h} - \sigma \,\mathrm{SE}(\bar{h}),
\]
where \(\bar{h}\) is the sample mean heavy-output probability across trials and \(\mathrm{SE}(\bar{h})\) is its standard error.

The reported Quantum Volume is
\[
QV = 2^{n_{\max}},
\]
where \(n_{\max}\) is the largest tested width that passes. Consequently, if the largest executed width also passes, the true quantum volume is at least this value and may in fact be larger.
\smallskip

For more details see \href{https://github.com/Classiq/classiq-library/tree/main/benchmarking/protocols/quantum\_volume\_protocol.ipynb}{Quantum Volume notebook}.
\medskip
